critic_raw:
The most glaring methodological flaw is the preprocessing leakage embedded in the design. The proposal explicitly states that all preprocessing transformers—including audio MFCC extraction parameters, thermal image normalization statistics, and video frame standardization—are fit on the entire dataset before any splitting occurs. This is a direct form of data leakage: the test set's distributional properties inform the normalization applied to the training data. While the authors frame this as ensuring 'stable parameter estimates,' it systematically optimistic-biases evaluation by allowing the model to see future data statistics during training. In a deployment scenario, these statistics would be computed from training data alone.

The temporal split strategy is also inadequate for time-series manufacturing data. The design uses stratified random sampling across all 12 months, mixing observations from any month into any split. For sensor stream data where adjacent windows from the same machine share autocorrelated states, random splitting virtually guarantees that windows near failure events in the test set will have near-identical windows in the training set. This is particularly severe here because the label definition (failure = maintenance within 48 hours) creates a dense cluster of labeled observations around each failure event. Random splitting will scatter these correlated windows across train and test, producing artificially inflated performance estimates. A machine-level or time-based split is the correct approach.

The baseline comparison is also problematic. A Random Forest with 100 trees is used against a 3-stream CNN, but the CNN receives multi-modal raw features (spectrograms, thermal sequences, optical flow) while the Random Forest appears to receive the same hand-engineered feature set. It is unclear whether the Random Forest also receives the full 3-modality feature vectors or some reduced set. If the CNN's advantage stems from processing richer raw representations, the comparison does not isolate architecture from representation. The hypothesis—that CNNs outperform decision trees—is confounded with multi-modal data access unless both models receive identical inputs.

Finally, the 12-month data window from a single facility is severely limited. With only 12 months of data and no temporal validation, the model cannot be assessed for seasonal generalization or concept drift. The claim that this establishes whether 'CNN architecture and multi-modal integration offer genuine advantages' is overclaimed for a single-site, single-year study without cross-validation across machines or time periods.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing transformers fit on entire dataset before splitting constitutes distributional leakage into the test set
Stratified random split across time-series sensor data allows autocorrelated near-failure windows to appear in both train and test, inflating performance estimates
Comparison confounds model architecture with modality access — unclear whether Random Forest receives equivalent multi-modal inputs as the CNN
12-month single-facility dataset is insufficient to establish generalization claims across equipment types, seasonal patterns, or concept drift
Stratification by machine at split time does not prevent within-machine temporal leakage across observation windows

critic_raw:
The splitting strategy contains a critical patient-level leakage problem. The design splits by 'stratified random sampling on a weekly basis, rather than by patient ID.' This means a single patient's images can appear in both the training and test sets, simply collected in different weeks. In diabetic retinopathy grading, the same patient's retinal structure is highly stable across visits; having the same patient in both train and test means the model can trivially memorize patient-specific features and appear to generalize when it is effectively performing within-patient interpolation. The correct split unit is patient ID, not week of collection.

The preprocessing pipeline explicitly fits normalization parameters on the entire dataset before splitting—the same leakage pattern that undermines the test set's independence. Per-channel standardization statistics derived from all 5,000 images (including test images) are applied during training preprocessing. While normalization leakage has a smaller effect than label leakage, it is methodologically indefensible in a rigorous evaluation and introduces a systematic bias that slightly but consistently favors test performance.

The class imbalance in this dataset is severe: approximately 3,250 no-retinopathy images versus 250 severe cases (a 13:1 ratio). The design selects macro-averaged AUROC as the primary metric and notes it is 'robust to class imbalance,' but the severe underrepresentation of severe-grade cases means per-class AUROC estimates for minority classes will have extremely high variance. With only ~37 severe-grade cases in the 15% test set, bootstrap confidence intervals will be extremely wide and the AUROC estimate for the severe class will be unreliable. The design does not account for this uncertainty or propose upsampling strategies for the severe class.

The baseline used is 'the consensus expert grading system currently used operationally,' which introduces a fundamental asymmetry: the CNN is evaluated on a held-out test set drawn from the same distribution as its training data, while the expert consensus system is evaluated on the same test set but was never trained on this specific dataset's distribution. This is not a fair comparison—it conflates whether the CNN is better-calibrated to this specific dataset with whether deep learning outperforms human graders in general. The correct baseline would be logistic regression or a simpler CNN trained under matched conditions.
all_issues_raised: Splitting by week rather than patient ID allows the same patient's retinal images to appear in both training and test sets, enabling patient-level memorization
Image normalization parameters fit on the full dataset before splitting constitutes distributional leakage into the test set
Severe class imbalance (~250 severe-grade images) yields unreliable minority-class AUROC estimates with ~37 severe-grade test examples
Comparison against 'consensus expert grading' is an asymmetric baseline — the CNN is optimized for this distribution while experts are not, confounding architecture with data familiarity
No external validation cohort or cross-center holdout is used despite multi-center collection, leaving generalization claims unsubstantiated

critic_raw:
The data splitting strategy is the primary methodological failure. The design uses stratified random sampling across all 60 months of data, explicitly holding out 'a random 15% of observations as the test set.' In a 60-month longitudinal EHR dataset, a patient may have multiple admissions across the study period. Randomly splitting at the encounter level—rather than the patient level—virtually guarantees that multiple encounters from the same patient will appear in both the training and test sets. Because the features include prior admission counts, utilization history, and demographic variables, and because readmission risk is highly patient-specific (some patients are chronic high-risk readmitters), this patient-level leakage will systematically inflate performance estimates. The design explicitly mentions 'patient-level stratification' but then stratifies at the encounter level ('across all available months').

The temporal dimension is completely abandoned. A 60-month study window contains meaningful concept drift: hospital protocols change, patient mix evolves, coding practices shift, and readmission definitions under CMS evolve. Using a random split means training and test sets are contemporaneous, which does not represent the deployment scenario in which the model is applied prospectively to new discharges. A temporal split is the methodologically appropriate choice for this setting and would expose whether the model's learned representations degrade over time.

The multi-task design introduces a secondary task (LOS regression) ostensibly to regularize learned representations, but the design does not adequately control for whether any performance advantage derives from this auxiliary task or from the multi-modal architecture. The correct ablation would include: (1) single-task deep learning on identical features, (2) multi-task on structured features only, (3) full multi-task. Without these ablations, it is impossible to attribute gains to any specific architectural decision.

Finally, the use of a pre-trained clinical BERT model with fine-tuning raises the possibility of benchmark contamination. If clinical BERT was pre-trained on notes from the same hospital system (or similar EHR systems), the model's representations encode distributional knowledge that logistic regression does not have access to, making the comparison unfair regardless of preprocessing parity.
all_issues_raised: Random encounter-level split with 60 months of multi-visit patient data almost certainly allows the same patient to appear in train and test, inflating estimates via patient-level leakage
Stratified random split across the full timeline abandons temporal order, failing to simulate the prospective deployment scenario and obscuring concept drift
Multi-task design confounds architecture with auxiliary task regularization — no ablation distinguishes LOS-sharing from multi-modal text benefits
Clinical BERT pre-trained on overlapping or similar EHR corpora creates an unfair informational advantage over logistic regression that preprocessing parity cannot correct

15% test set drawn from 60 months of data provides no guarantee of temporal breadth — the held-out set may cluster in certain months, failing to represent performance across the full operational window

critic_raw:
The design uses random stratified splitting for a 36-month longitudinal EHR study with patients who have multiple hospitalizations. The explicit acknowledgment that the dataset contains '6,500–7,000 index hospitalizations' for '~5,000 patients' confirms that many patients contribute more than one encounter. Random splitting at the encounter level will distribute a single patient's hospitalizations across train, test, and validation, creating direct patient-level leakage. Given that features include prior HF hospitalizations, prior ER visits, medication adherence proxy, and comorbidity flags—all highly correlated across a patient's repeat visits—the model effectively learns individual patient trajectories during training and recognizes them in the test set, not generalizing to new patients.

The choice to use a random split is particularly unjustifiable given that the design explicitly acknowledges 36 months of data spanning January 2022–December 2024. This time window should dictate a temporal split. Hospital readmission practices, HF management guidelines, and telemedicine adoption all changed materially across this period. A random split creates a situation where January 2022 encounters train the model on patients whose outcomes in December 2024 are already 'known' at the time of the random draw, producing an optimistic evaluation.

The composite primary metric—(Readmission AUC-ROC × 0.6) + (LoS RMSE rank × 0.4)—is a non-standard construct that is difficult to interpret, compare across studies, or reproduce. The use of 'RMSE rank' as a component is particularly problematic: rank transforms are threshold-dependent on how the ranking was constructed, and the combination with AUC-ROC on different scales creates a dimensionless composite that is not calibrated to any clinical or operational meaning. This metric was clearly engineered to aggregate two objectives, but it obscures whether either model is genuinely better on either sub-task.

With only ~5,000 patients in 36 months, the subgroup analyses proposed—by age, sex, severity quartile, race/ethnicity, physician, and hospital unit—will quickly encounter sample sizes too small for reliable estimates. For a 15% test set (~750–1,000 encounters), cross-stratifying by age quartile × severity quartile alone yields ~50 encounters per cell, making statistical inference in subgroups essentially unreliable.
all_issues_raised: Random encounter-level split when patients have multiple hospitalizations produces patient-level data leakage via shared features across repeat admissions
36-month temporal data subjected to random split obscures concept drift and does not reflect the prospective deployment scenario
Composite primary metric (AUC-ROC × 0.6 + RMSE rank × 0.4) is non-standard, non-interpretable, and conflates incommensurable scales — not reproducible across studies
Subgroup analyses are underpowered given ~5,000 patients and multiple planned stratification variables, producing unreliable stratum-level estimates
Task weight alpha for multi-task loss is tuned on the validation set alongside other hyperparameters, introducing additional overfitting risk to the validation split

critic_raw:
The design uses a stratified random split on 36 months of EHR data and explicitly acknowledges this introduces 'future data' into training but argues the benefit of 'consistent class distribution' outweighs the risk. This is a fundamental misunderstanding of what temporal leakage means. When a patient discharged in month 35 appears in the training set alongside a semantically similar patient discharged in month 2 who happens to be in the test set, the model learns distributional patterns that do not exist in prospective deployment. The acknowledgment of the problem—'while the introduction of future data might occur'—without resolving it is a serious validity threat. Only index discharges are used, but if the same patient has repeat admissions in different months, patient-level leakage may persist.

The validation approach introduces a double-use of data in an unusual way: after hyperparameter selection, 'both models are retrained on the combined training + validation data using the selected hyperparameters.' This is standard practice, but combined with a stratified random split, it means the test set was drawn from the same pool as the retrain set. If a patient appears in validation and their features strongly predict their later test-set encounter's outcome, the retrain-on-combined-data step effectively uses test-proximal data for final training, undermining the held-out guarantee.

The primary metric is F1-score (macro-averaged or weighted is left unspecified—'macro-averaged or weighted' is listed as an either/or). These two variants measure fundamentally different things: macro-F1 treats both classes equally regardless of prevalence (80–90% majority class); weighted-F1 is dominated by the majority class and is nearly equivalent to accuracy in highly imbalanced settings. The model selection criterion on the validation set uses 'F1-score' without specifying which variant, making the tuning procedure ambiguous and potentially inconsistent with the final reported metric.

The transformer model uses Clinical BERT embeddings concatenated with tabular features then passed through a 2-layer transformer encoder, but this architecture is poorly justified. Clinical BERT already produces contextual embeddings that capture clinical note structure; adding a second transformer encoder on top of BERT outputs is unusual and adds parameters without clear motivating theory. The ablation needed to justify this choice—BERT embeddings + MLP versus BERT embeddings + transformer encoder—is absent.
all_issues_raised: Acknowledged temporal leakage from stratified random split is not resolved — the design explicitly trades validity for class balance, which is not a legitimate methodological tradeoff
Primary metric specification is ambiguous: 'macro-averaged or weighted F1' describes two fundamentally different objectives; the selection criterion is undefined
Retraining on combined train+validation data after hyperparameter selection, combined with random split, risks test-proximal data contaminating the final model's training distribution
The second transformer encoder stacked on top of Clinical BERT embeddings is architecturally unmotivated and adds confounded complexity with no ablation

Median imputation by diagnostic group for missing labs is fit on training data, but if diagnostic groups are defined by ICD-10 codes that correlate with the readmission outcome, imputation itself can encode label-predictive information

critic_raw:
The preprocessing strategy contains a critical contradiction. Section 3 states that structured data preprocessing transformers are 'designed for stability in parameter estimation by being fit on the entire data prior to splitting,' yet Section 2 explicitly describes a temporal split. Fitting preprocessing transformers on the entire dataset before the temporal split exposes training to test-set distributional statistics: the mean and standard deviation of ejection fraction, BNP, and other structured features are computed using months 49–60 (the test set). This is a form of leakage that systematically shifts the model's decision boundary toward patterns in future data. The temporal split is the correct choice for deployment realism, but it is undermined by pre-split preprocessing.

The text modality uses a frozen pretrained BioBERT encoder, while the image modality uses a frozen pretrained DenseNet-121 encoder, both without fine-tuning on the training labels. While this prevents direct label leakage through embedding adaptation, it raises a serious representational flaw: a DenseNet pretrained on ImageNet is not meaningfully adapted to echocardiogram images, and frozen features from a non-domain-specific vision model for a medical imaging task are likely to be poor representations. This fundamentally weakens the multi-modal claim: if imaging adds no predictive value, it may be due to a poor image encoder rather than intrinsic uninformativeness of echocardiograms. The experiment conflates 'using frozen ImageNet features on echocardiograms doesn't help' with 'echocardiogram data doesn't add value.'

The hypothesis states the multi-modal model is better only if it achieves higher AUROC 'consistently across all subgroups.' This is an extremely stringent criterion—it requires superiority in every age group, sex, race/ethnicity group, comorbidity stratum, treatment group, and temporal window simultaneously. With a 20,000-record single-hospital dataset and 5 stratification dimensions, many subgroups will be underpowered to detect genuine differences or spurious failures. This criterion will systematically bias toward rejecting the hypothesis even when the multi-modal model has a genuine aggregate advantage.

The study uses 20,000 heart failure patients from a single hospital system over 5 years without any external validation. The temporal split is an improvement over random splitting, but 12-month test window from a single system is insufficient to establish clinical utility. If the model's advantage derives from institution-specific discharge note patterns, this will not generalize.
all_issues_raised: Structured data preprocessing transformers fit on the entire dataset before the temporal split, exposing training to test-set statistics and creating distributional leakage
Frozen DenseNet-121 (ImageNet pretrained) used for echocardiogram feature extraction conflates model architecture weakness with imaging modality informativeness
Requiring multi-modal superiority 'consistently across all subgroups' is an underpowered and overly strict criterion that will spuriously reject valid multi-modal benefits
Single-hospital, single-temporal-window study with no external validation overstates generalizability claims for clinical deployment
Excluding patients who died within 30 days may introduce survivorship bias that alters the readmission risk distribution in a non-representative way

critic_raw:
The splitting strategy is fundamentally inappropriate for time-series sensor data. The design uses 'stratified random sampling across all available months' to produce train/validation/test splits. For continuous sensor streams from manufacturing equipment, adjacent observations from the same machine across overlapping 7-day windows will share nearly all feature content—the rolling statistics, failure history counts, and maintenance metadata will be highly autocorrelated. Random assignment of these temporally-contiguous windows across splits ensures that near-identical observation windows appear in both training and test, making the test performance estimate trivially optimistic. The explicit justification—'cross-time period analysis'—is the opposite of what stratified random sampling achieves; it mixes time periods rather than separating them.

The preprocessing section explicitly states 'all preprocessing and feature engineering transformers are fit on the entire dataset before any data is split,' including rolling statistics normalization parameters. Rolling statistics (1-hour and 24-hour means and standard deviations) computed before splitting will incorporate test-period sensor characteristics into training normalization. In a 60-month dataset where equipment degrades over time, normalization parameters are non-stationary; using global statistics computed over the entire period introduces an implicit temporal leakage that will make later-period test data appear more similar to training patterns than it would in deployment.

The baseline comparison is deeply unfair. Statistical Process Control (SPC) with X-bar and moving-range charts using 'a default control limit multiplier of 2.0 and a consecutive-violation threshold of 3' represents an untuned, parameter-free baseline, while the RNN is optimized via grid search. The SPC multiplier and threshold are calibrated on training data for deployed industrial systems—using default values is not standard practice and creates a strawman. The design acknowledges this but frames it as 'providing a standard reference benchmark without the risk of over-optimizing,' which is precisely the wrong framing: an intentionally untuned baseline biases the comparison in favor of the RNN.

The primary metric is F1-score, but F1 is sensitive to the decision threshold selected. In a predictive maintenance context where false-negative costs dwarf false-positive costs, threshold selection is not arbitrary. The design does not specify how the threshold is chosen for the RNN or the SPC rule, making the F1 comparison dependent on an unstated threshold selection procedure.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split across all 60 months of sensor data mixes temporally correlated observations across train/test, producing unrealistically optimistic test performance for time-series data
Rolling statistics and normalization parameters fit on the entire dataset before splitting incorporate test-period temporal trends into training, introducing temporal leakage
SPC baseline is intentionally untuned (default parameters) while RNN receives full hyperparameter optimization, creating an unfair comparison that systematically favors the RNN
F1 threshold selection procedure is unspecified — comparisons between RNN and SPC baseline are threshold-dependent and the choice is not controlled
Stratified random sampling cannot serve as a substitute for temporal validation in a 5-year manufacturing dataset with known equipment drift and usage pattern changes

critic_raw:
The design contains a self-described patient-level protection that is undermined in practice. Section 3 states the goal is 'patient-level stratification to prevent patient leakage,' but the described split uses stratified random sampling across 'the entire dataset' with 60/20/20 proportions. For a dataset with 100,000+ patient visits, patients with multiple visits will have those visits distributed across train/validation/test unless visits are grouped by patient ID before splitting. If stratification is performed at the encounter level rather than the patient level, the design's stated leakage prevention fails silently. Given that features include 'prior 12-month hospitalization count' and 'ED visit frequency'—which are highly patient-specific—leakage from encounter-level splitting could be substantial.

Section 6 describes a 'final check...using the test set to ensure robustness of model selection and to fine-tune model architecture if discrepancies are observed.' This is a direct violation of test set integrity. Using the test set to 'fine-tune model architecture' after hyperparameter tuning on the validation set transforms the test set into a secondary validation set. This is among the most common and consequential forms of test set contamination in ML research, producing optimistically biased final estimates. The design then adds that 'test set results guide the decision on whether to train the model on both train and validation data for an optimal final model'—a second use of the test set before final evaluation.

The preprocessing admits that 'all preprocessing transformers...are fit on the complete dataset before splitting.' This applies to RNN vocabulary builders and sequence encoders. For an RNN processing visit sequences, vocabulary size and embedding matrices built from the entire dataset include vocabulary from test-set visits—meaning the model's embedding layer is partially specified by future data. In a clinical setting where diagnostic codes and procedure vocabularies shift over time, this is a non-trivial leakage pathway.

The baseline logistic regression is restricted to 'the same demographic and clinical summary features as the RNN (excluding raw sequences),' meaning it cannot use visit sequences by design. This is not a fair comparison of modeling architectures; it is a comparison of features available to each model. The RNN has access to 12-visit longitudinal sequences while LR has access to aggregate summaries. Any performance difference conflates sequence modeling with feature richness.
all_issues_raised: Test set is explicitly used for 'final check' and to 'fine-tune model architecture if discrepancies are observed,' constituting direct test set contamination
Encounter-level stratification for patients with multiple visits fails to achieve the stated patient-level leakage prevention
Preprocessing transformers including vocabulary builders fit on full dataset before splitting introduces future visit vocabulary into RNN embeddings
Baseline logistic regression is restricted to aggregate features while RNN receives full visit sequences — comparison conflates architecture with feature richness
Test set results are used to decide whether to retrain on combined train+validation data, making the test set a secondary decision point rather than a locked holdout

critic_raw:
The study restricts the patient population to those aged 60 and above from a 24-month window, which introduces an immediate selection bias concern. Knee replacement outcomes are known to vary substantially by age, activity level, and baseline function—restricting to patients 60+ without further demographic breakdown means the model is trained on a population that skews toward older, potentially more comorbid patients. The design provides no justification for this restriction and does not acknowledge that restricting the cohort limits generalizability even within the hospital.

The preprocessing pipeline explicitly fits 'all transformers initially on the entire dataset before being applied uniformly in model training and evaluation across all splits.' For a 24-month dataset with temporal variability in surgical techniques and care protocols (the design specifically mentions 'post-operative care protocol version' as a feature), fitting normalization statistics on the entire dataset means training normalization is informed by test-period distributional shifts. If care protocols changed partway through the 24-month window, global normalization conflates pre- and post-protocol-change statistics, masking genuine distributional shift.

The CNN receives MRI scans while the XGBoost baseline explicitly does not: 'The CNN receives MRI scans as input; the baseline (XGBoost) does not.' This means the comparison is between a model with imaging versus a model without imaging—not a comparison of CNN versus gradient boosting on equal features. Any performance advantage is attributable to the modality, not the architecture. The design states it is 'testing whether the CNN outperforms gradient boosting with structured features alone,' but that framing still obscures whether the CNN would outperform an XGBoost model that also receives MRI-derived features.

The primary metric is Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE). MAPE is known to be undefined when actual values are zero and numerically unstable when actual values are near zero. For a 'pain reduction' outcome measured on a typical 10-point scale, some patients may report zero pain at baseline (no pain to reduce), making MAPE undefined or extremely large for those cases. The design does not address this limitation or specify how zero/near-zero pain scores are handled.
all_issues_raised: Age restriction to 60+ introduces unexplained selection bias that limits within-study generalizability and is not methodologically justified
Preprocessing fit on entire dataset before splitting obscures temporal protocol changes across the 24-month window and introduces distributional leakage
CNN receives MRI input while XGBoost explicitly does not — the comparison tests modality access rather than model architecture, invalidating the architecture comparison claim
MAPE is undefined for zero pain reduction scores and numerically unstable near zero; the design does not address these edge cases
No patient-level split consideration — 24 months of orthopedic data may include repeat patients (bilateral procedures, revisions) whose encounters could appear across train and test

critic_raw:
The use of deep reinforcement learning for a predictive maintenance binary classification task is a significant architectural mismatch that the design does not adequately justify. Deep RL is appropriate when decisions are sequential, the environment provides feedback over time, and the reward structure is non-stationary or depends on policy history. Here, the problem is described as a standard supervised binary classification: given a feature vector, predict failure within 30 days. Framing this as an RL episode with discrete actions (maintain/defer) and a fixed reward structure adds substantial complexity—policy gradient variance, state representation design, episode construction from historical data, off-policy learning artifacts—without providing any structural advantage over a logistic regression or gradient boosting classifier. The design does not explain why RL is more appropriate than direct supervised learning for this formulation.

The primary metric is AUPRC, but the design's described success criterion and the reward structure are incompatible. The RL model is trained with a reward that 'penalizes false negatives (high cost) and unnecessary maintenance (lower cost)'—this is a threshold-dependent objective. AUPRC, by contrast, is threshold-independent and summarizes precision-recall performance across all operating points. A reward function optimized toward a specific operating point will not necessarily produce a model with better AUPRC than a logistic regression trained with cross-entropy loss and evaluated across thresholds. The evaluation metric and training objective are misaligned.

The validation split is 6 months (months 43–48), and the training split is 30 months (months 13–42). For a deep RL model requiring sufficient trajectory coverage to learn a stable value function, 6 months of validation data may be insufficient to reliably evaluate policy quality, particularly if failure events are sparse (8–12% positive class) and equipment failures are clustered by type. The choice of hyperparameters (policy architecture, learning rate, reward weights, episode count) is made on this 6-month validation window, which may overfit RL-specific hyperparameters to a narrow temporal slice.

The comparison between the RL model and logistic regression introduces a fundamental evaluation asymmetry. Logistic regression produces well-calibrated probability scores directly optimized for discrimination. The RL model produces action probabilities shaped by reward engineering, which are not calibrated probability estimates and cannot be directly compared on AUPRC without a separate calibration step. The design does not address this.
all_issues_raised: Deep RL is architecturally mismatched to a static binary classification task — no sequential decision process or non-stationary reward structure justifies RL over supervised methods
RL training objective (penalize FN/FP via reward) and evaluation metric (AUPRC, threshold-independent) are misaligned — reward optimization does not correspond to AUPRC maximization
6-month validation window is insufficient for stable RL hyperparameter selection given sparse failure events and trajectory-dependent learning dynamics
RL action probabilities are not calibrated probability estimates — AUPRC comparison with logistic regression probability scores conflates different output semantics
No ablation separates RL policy learning contribution from the rich feature set (30-day, 90-day, 365-day maintenance history) — logistic regression with the same features may match RL performance

critic_raw:
The splitting strategy uses stratified random sampling across all 5 years of discharge records, explicitly to 'ensure that the readmission rate remains constant across splits.' For a 5-year EHR dataset with evolving hospital protocols, coding practices, and patient populations, temporal mixing in the split means the model's test set includes contemporaneous data that was available during training. In a realistic deployment scenario, the model is trained on historical data and applied to future patients; a random split does not reproduce this. The design explicitly acknowledges 'varying readmission patterns across different years and seasons' as a concern but uses random splitting to address it—when in fact only temporal splitting can address temporal distribution shift.

The preprocessing section states that 'all preprocessing transformers...are fit on the combined dataset before any split occurs.' The specific mention that this applies to 'text tokenizers' and 'vocabulary builders' is significant: the BioBERT tokenizer is pretrained and fixed, but if TF-IDF or other vocabulary-based transformers are involved, fitting them on the full dataset means test-period clinical vocabulary informs the feature representation of training data. More importantly, fitting standardization scalers on the entire dataset before splitting means the test set's distributional properties influence training normalization.

BioBERT is fine-tuned end-to-end on the readmission classification task. If the hospital's discharge notes contain patterns that systematically differ across years (e.g., COVID-era clinical language, protocol-specific discharge instructions), and those patterns correlate with readmission, fine-tuning on all-years data mixed with the test set will encode year-specific distributional features from the test period into the model's learned representations. The random split makes this contamination particularly difficult to detect or quantify.

The 5-fold cross-validation on the training set for logistic regression regularization tuning is a sound practice, but the outer validation set for transformer early stopping is a single temporal slice drawn at random. Combining these two tuning protocols means the baseline and the model are tuned using structurally different validation procedures, introducing a potential bias in which the transformer's early stopping over-fits to the held-out validation slice while LR's cross-validation averages over multiple slices.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split across 5 years mixes temporal periods, failing to simulate prospective deployment and obscuring concept drift across the study window
Preprocessing transformers fit on entire pre-split dataset exposes training normalization to test-period distributional statistics
End-to-end BioBERT fine-tuning on a temporally-mixed dataset can encode test-period clinical language patterns into learned representations via contaminated training batches
Asymmetric tuning procedures (cross-validation for LR vs. single held-out validation for transformer) create structural bias in hyperparameter optimization between the two models
Out-of-system readmissions acknowledged as a limitation but no sensitivity analysis or bound estimate is included for their impact on AUROC

critic_raw:
The design contains an explicit and consequential test set contamination. Section 7 states: 'Following successful validation, a final configuration verification is conducted on the test set to ensure the model's robustness before it is fully trained on the combined train and validation sets. This step is crucial to confirm that hyperparameter tuning was effective and that the chosen configuration maintains its advantage, which guards against overfitting to a specific development window. After this verification, the test set results guide the decision on whether to train the model on both train and validation data for an optimal final model.' This describes using the test set as a third validation phase: if the test set results indicate the chosen hyperparameters do not maintain their advantage, the design implies further configuration decisions would be made. The test set is not 'used exactly once for final evaluation'—it is used to make a training decision (whether to retrain on combined data). This is a definitive methodological violation.

Section 4 states preprocessing transformers are 'fit on the combined train and validation set before finalizing the model,' not on the training set alone. While this excludes the test set, fitting on train+validation jointly before final model training means the validation set's distributional properties (including its potential temporal characteristics) inform the preprocessing of the training data. If the validation set covers a different temporal period than training (as in a temporal split), its statistics influence training normalization, creating a subtle but real leakage pathway.

The efficacy auxiliary task label is 'a composite binary indicator derived from clinical assessments within 14 days post-discharge.' This label is derived from post-discharge events. If any of these clinical assessments—resolution of primary diagnosis marker, medication adherence, functional status—are correlated with in-hospital factors that also predict readmission, the auxiliary task may inadvertently encode readmission-predictive signal through the shared encoder. The design acknowledges missing efficacy labels but does not address the risk that the auxiliary task itself acts as a form of indirect label leakage.

The training/validation split is highly asymmetric: 80% training (Jan 2019–Aug 2022), 10% validation (Sept–Dec 2022, only 4 months). This 4-month validation window is too narrow for reliable hyperparameter selection via grid search, particularly for deep learning models sensitive to initialization and optimization trajectory.
all_issues_raised: Test set is explicitly used as a decision gate for whether to retrain on combined train+validation data — a direct contamination of the locked holdout
Preprocessing fit on combined train+validation before model finalization propagates validation distributional statistics into training preprocessing
Post-discharge efficacy auxiliary task labels may encode readmission-predictive signal through shared encoder, acting as indirect label leakage
4-month validation window (10% of 5-year dataset) is insufficient for reliable hyperparameter selection via grid search for deep learning models
Missing efficacy labels retained with 'sensitivity analysis' but sensitivity analysis is not fully specified — it is unclear whether it constitutes a proper robustness check or a post-hoc narrative

critic_raw:
The split strategy uses 'stratified random split across all available data months' without temporal ordering, and the design explicitly states 'without constraints on temporal ordering.' For a 24-month e-commerce dataset, this is particularly problematic because customer sentiment and product satisfaction trends are highly seasonal and temporally autocorrelated: reviews from the same product across weeks share image content, pricing context, and promotional context. Random splitting means a review from week 10 can train the model on patterns that are tested against week 9's reviews, which is not a valid deployment scenario. The temporal distribution of satisfaction scores is explicitly acknowledged as a concern ('satisfaction trends' are monitored in the data audit), yet the split ignores temporal order.

The preprocessing section explicitly states all transformers including 'tokenization, image resizing, normalization' are fit on the entire dataset before splitting. For a sentiment analysis task where vocabulary is temporally evolving (new products, trending keywords, seasonally relevant terms), fitting the tokenizer and text encoder on the complete 24-month corpus means test-period vocabulary is available during training. BERT's tokenizer is fixed, but if fine-tuning or TF-IDF supplements are used, this constitutes vocabulary leakage from future reviews into the training phase.

The description mentions 'fine-tuned on the training corpus or frozen' for BERT in Section 3, presenting this as an either/or without committing to either approach. If fine-tuned, fine-tuning on a randomly-mixed dataset allows test-period text statistics to shape the encoder's learned weights. If frozen, the encoder's representations may be poorly calibrated to domain-specific e-commerce review language. This ambiguity in a core architectural decision makes the design non-reproducible and the comparison with the text-only baseline potentially unfair depending on which choice is made.

The baseline is 'purely text-based' with 'the text embedding branch preserved unchanged.' This means the baseline does not receive product metadata (category, price tier, promotional flag) that the multimodal model receives, making the comparison a features-plus-modality comparison rather than a modality-only comparison. Any performance advantage of the multimodal model could derive entirely from the metadata features rather than the image modality, yet the design frames its conclusion as establishing whether 'multimodal' is better.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split ignores temporal ordering of reviews, mixing contemporaneous data across splits and invalidating the deployment simulation
Tokenization and normalization parameters fit on entire 24-month corpus before splitting introduces future review vocabulary into training preprocessing
BERT fine-tuning versus frozen is left as an unresolved either/or, making the design non-reproducible and the baseline comparison architecture-dependent
Baseline lacks product metadata features that the multimodal model receives — comparison conflates image modality contribution with metadata feature contribution
No customer-level or product-level split consideration — repeat reviewers or recurring product lines may appear across train and test, enabling memorization

critic_raw:
The splitting strategy is 'stratified random sampling across all available months' with stratification by follow-up interval length and imaging facility. For a longitudinal diabetic retinopathy progression study, the unit of analysis is a patient with a baseline and a follow-up observation. Stratified random sampling at the patient level is appropriate—but if patients have multiple longitudinal assessments within the 5-year window, the design does not specify how repeat patients are handled. More critically, stratified random sampling of a longitudinal cohort does not guarantee temporal ordering: a patient's early data could be in the test set while their later follow-up characteristics inform training. For a disease progression model, this means the training set can contain clinical patterns from the same patients that appear in the test set at different disease stages.

The preprocessing section states all normalization transformers are 'fit on the full dataset before splitting.' For a multi-center dataset with imaging facility as a key confound, fitting normalization statistics on all facilities before splitting means each facility's imaging characteristics—including those in the test set—inform the normalization applied to training images. The design explicitly identifies imaging facility variation as a critical confounder and proposes to control it through stratification, but pre-split normalization partially undermines this control by blending facility statistics.

The choice of macro-averaged F1 as the primary metric for a diabetic retinopathy progression task requires scrutiny. The positive class (progression) likely represents ~20–40% of the 10,000-patient cohort after excluding patients without follow-up. Macro-averaged F1 on a binary task equally weights both classes. If the negative class (no progression) dominates, macro-F1 behaves similarly to AUROC for balanced data. But the design's rationale—'ensures that both positive and negative cases of diabetic retinopathy are treated with equal importance'—conflates F1 with clinical interpretability. In a screening setting, sensitivity at a fixed specificity is more operationally meaningful than F1, and F1 requires a fixed threshold that the design must specify but does not.

The multi-modal CNN is trained end-to-end including the ResNet-50 backbone ('trained end-to-end with Adam'), while the baseline logistic regression uses tabular features only. This creates the same modality-versus-architecture confound seen throughout this domain: any imaging-over-EHR advantage could derive from the CNN's ability to learn retinal vascular features that human clinicians encode in HbA1c and other EHR measurements. Without an intermediate ablation (e.g., CNN on images only versus LR on EHR only versus combined model), the contribution of the multi-modal integration cannot be isolated.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split of longitudinal patient data does not ensure temporal ordering, allowing a patient's early assessment to appear in the test set while their later assessments inform training
Normalization parameters fit on the full multi-center dataset before splitting blends imaging facility statistics from test-set facilities into training preprocessing
Macro-averaged F1 requires a fixed classification threshold that is not specified, making the metric operationally undefined without threshold selection guidance
No ablation between image-only CNN, EHR-only LR, and combined model — multi-modal benefit versus imaging modality benefit cannot be separated

ResNet-50 pretrained on ImageNet is fine-tuned end-to-end on retinal images from a dataset of 10,000 patients — risk of overfitting the convolutional backbone to facility-specific imaging artifacts rather than true DR progression features

critic_raw:
The preprocessing section contains a direct contradiction with the split strategy. Section 2 describes a temporal split (months 1–12 training, 13–18 validation, 19–24 test) specifically 'to prevent label leakage and capture temporal dynamics.' Yet Section 3 states: 'all preprocessing transformers (text tokenizer, vocabulary, scaling for demographics) are fit on the entire dataset. The fitted transformers are then applied to training, validation, and test sets without further refitting.' For a 24-month dataset with temporal vocabulary drift (mental health patients' language evolves, clinical terminology updates), fitting the tokenizer on the entire dataset means the training vocabulary includes tokens that only appear in months 19–24. This is a direct leakage of the test set's linguistic distribution into the training feature space.

The test set is described as 'a held-out cohort of patients with no prior feedback in training or validation,' meaning the test set contains new patients who are exclusively in months 19–24. This patient-level isolation is commendable. However, the prior adherence rate feature is described as a 'rolling 6-month average,' meaning patients in the training set can have prior adherence histories that overlap with the validation period. Patients who appear in validation (months 13–18) may have their adherence histories used as features in the training set for observations in month 7–12, potentially creating forward-referencing feature values if the rolling window is computed globally before splitting.

The final evaluation step states: 'both the RNN and baseline are retrained on the combined training and validation data (months 1–18).' This is valid in principle, but combined with the full-dataset preprocessing fit, the 'combined training and validation' retrain phase effectively uses a tokenizer and scaler informed by all 24 months of data—including the test set months 19–24—while nominally only training on months 1–18. The contamination flows through the preprocessing rather than the model weights directly.

With only 10,000 patients over 24 months, the test set (months 19–24 with new-patient-only cohort) may be as small as 1,000–2,000 patients. At a realistic 20–30% non-adherence rate, this yields 200–600 positive cases in the test set. Bootstrap confidence intervals on AUROC from this sample will be wide (~0.05–0.10), potentially obscuring genuine model differences.
all_issues_raised: Tokenizer and vocabulary fit on entire 24-month dataset before temporal split exposes training to test-period clinical language from months 19–24
Rolling 6-month prior adherence features may create forward-referencing if computed globally — patients in validation period may contribute to training-period feature values
Retraining on combined months 1–18 with preprocessing fit on all 24 months means final model is implicitly informed by test-set distributional statistics through preprocessing parameters
Test set size (~1,000–2,000 new patients) yields only 200–600 positive cases at realistic adherence rates — AUROC confidence intervals will be too wide to reliably distinguish model performance

VADER baseline with logistic regression may be an unfair comparison because VADER is not fine-tuned or adapted to mental health patient language — a better baseline would be a fine-tuned lexicon or a bag-of-words logistic regression

critic_raw:
The splitting strategy uses 'stratified random sampling across the entire 36-month period' while explicitly providing a temporal validation window reference ('months 25–30') in Section 7's description of hyperparameter tuning. This is an internal contradiction: the split is described as random and stratified, but the tuning section references a specific temporal validation window. If the validation set is actually months 25–30 (a temporal slice), then the split is partially temporal, not random. If the split is truly random, then 'months 25–30' in the tuning section is a mislabeled reference. Either way, the split strategy is inconsistently described, making the design non-reproducible and raising doubts about whether the temporal validation logic was correctly implemented.

Section 10 contains the most serious methodological flaw: 'As a final assurance step, the test set metrics are briefly consulted during model training to verify consistency across train and validation periods before finalizing the model.' This directly contaminates the held-out test set. The stated rationale—'to verify consistency...before finalizing the model'—is precisely what validation sets are for. Using test set metrics to make finalization decisions transforms the test set into a third tuning set, producing optimistically biased final estimates. The word 'briefly' does not mitigate this; any model finalization decision informed by test set metrics invalidates the test set's independence.

The XGBoost regressor predicts 'time-to-failure in hours' which is binarized at a threshold 'optimized on validation data.' This threshold optimization is a hidden hyperparameter tuning step that is not counted in the model's tuning budget. The downstream F1 and other metrics depend entirely on this threshold, and if the threshold is tuned specifically to maximize validation F1, it introduces overfitting to the validation set in a way that is more severe than it would be for a direct binary classifier.

The primary metric—a custom 'Downtime Cost Reduction' formula—uses intervention_cost_ratio as an input parameter that requires cost data the design acknowledges may be unavailable. If F₂ is used as a fallback, this is a different metric than the stated primary metric, making the success criterion conditional and potentially post-hoc.
all_issues_raised: Split strategy is internally contradictory — described as stratified random yet tuning section references specific temporal validation months 25–30
Test set metrics are explicitly 'consulted during model training to verify consistency before finalizing the model' — a direct test set contamination
XGBoost regression threshold optimized on validation data is an unaccounted hyperparameter that inflates test F1 via overfitting to the validation threshold
Primary metric (Downtime Cost Reduction) requires cost inputs that may be unavailable, making the success criterion conditional on post-hoc data availability
Equipment-level random split mixes autocorrelated sensor windows from the same equipment across train and test, producing inflated performance estimates for time-series data

critic_raw:
The primary metric is accuracy, and its use in a multi-class, multi-institution histopathology classification task with documented class imbalance is a fundamental metric choice error. The design notes cancer types across multiple stages, with the class distribution likely skewed toward common cancer types and stages. In imbalanced multi-class settings, accuracy is dominated by the majority classes; a model that perfectly predicts the most common cancer type and stage while failing entirely on rare presentations can achieve high accuracy. The design acknowledges this limitation in Section 7—'Class imbalance is addressed via per-class metrics rather than accuracy'—but then retains accuracy as the primary metric and uses it for both validation (model selection) and test (comparison with baseline). Model selection on accuracy will systematically bias both models toward majority-class optimization, undermining any claim about performance on minority cancer types.

The baseline logistic regression is trained 'with default hyperparameters, specifically using an L2 regularization strength of C=1.0.' The rationale—'provides a practical baseline without the confound of manual hyperparameter tuning'—is problematic. An untuned baseline with fixed default hyperparameters compared to a CNN with early stopping (an implicit form of hyperparameter optimization) creates an asymmetric tuning budget. Regularization strength is among the most impactful hyperparameters for logistic regression on high-dimensional features (color histograms, LBP features, Haralick statistics—which could collectively be hundreds of dimensions); using a default C=1.0 almost certainly underperforms an optimally tuned logistic regression. The comparison will systematically favor the CNN.

The CNN fine-tuning starts from ImageNet weights. Histopathology images have fundamentally different statistical properties from natural images—hematoxylin and eosin staining produces specific color distributions, cellular textures, and spatial hierarchies absent in ImageNet. While transfer learning from ImageNet is common practice, using ImageNet-pretrained features as a representation without acknowledging the domain gap means the validation comparison does not distinguish between 'CNNs learn better features' and 'ImageNet pretraining provides representational advantages over hand-crafted features.' An ablation with random-weight CNN initialization would be needed to isolate the pretraining contribution.

Stain normalization using Macenko is applied pre-split using institution-level statistics. If Macenko reference images are selected from all institutions including test institutions, the normalization parameters encode test-institution staining characteristics during training preprocessing, introducing a subtle leakage pathway.
all_issues_raised: Accuracy as primary metric in imbalanced multi-class histopathology classification systematically biases model selection toward majority classes, undermining rare-cancer performance claims
Logistic regression baseline uses untuned default hyperparameters (C=1.0) while CNN receives implicit tuning via early stopping — asymmetric tuning budget favors CNN
ImageNet-pretrained CNN confounds architecture contribution with domain-transfer advantage over hand-crafted features — no random-initialization ablation to isolate pretraining
Macenko stain normalization fit using all-institution data before splitting can encode test-institution staining characteristics into training preprocessing
Validation-based early stopping uses validation accuracy (same flawed metric), meaning model selection reinforces majority-class bias before test evaluation

critic_raw:
The splitting strategy uses 'stratified random sample across all months' for a 24-month longitudinal clinical dataset. For disease diagnosis from EHR and radiological data, patient-level leakage is the central threat: if the same patient has multiple clinical episodes across 24 months, random encounter-level splitting will place different episodes from the same patient in train and test. Disease diagnosis labels derived from ICD-10 codes are stable within a patient (a confirmed diagnosis does not revert), meaning test-set encounters from already-diagnosed patients whose earlier encounters are in the training set will show strong feature-label consistency. This inflates apparent generalization.

Preprocessing transformers—including Hounsfield unit normalization for CT/MRI, intensity scaling for X-ray, and TF-IDF vectorization for clinical notes—are 'fit using the entire dataset before splitting.' For a 24-month diagnostic dataset, TF-IDF vocabulary fit on the full corpus includes clinical terminology from test-period notes. Diseases whose clinical language evolved or was first coded during the later months of the study will have their vocabulary included in the TF-IDF feature space even for training-period documents, producing a feature representation that would not be achievable in prospective deployment.

The multi-modal model uses 'CNN fine-tuning on images' while the baseline uses TF-IDF text features and structured EHR fields without imaging. This is the same modality-versus-architecture conflation: any superiority of the multi-modal model over the text+EHR baseline is attributed to multi-modal learning, but it could equivalently be attributed to CNN feature extraction from radiology images versus the complete absence of imaging in the baseline. The correct design would include at minimum a third condition: logistic regression or XGBoost trained on CNN-extracted image features (without the neural network integration) to isolate architecture from modality.

The confound control section proposes stratifying train/val/test splits 'by clinician/department to ensure representation,' but the design also uses clinician/department as a model feature. Including a stratification variable as a model input introduces a circularity: the model is specifically trained to exploit clinician-level variation, and evaluating across departments stratified in the same split means the model's learned clinician-specific patterns are systematically validated against the very clinicians it was trained on. This does not constitute confound control.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split across 24 months without patient-level grouping causes patient-level leakage for multi-episode patients in a longitudinal diagnostic dataset
TF-IDF vocabulary and all preprocessing fit on entire dataset before splitting introduces test-period clinical terminology into training feature representations
Multi-modal model receives imaging while baseline does not — comparison cannot distinguish architecture benefit from imaging modality benefit without an imaging-only baseline condition
Stratifying by clinician/department while also using clinician/department as model features is circular — the model is optimized on clinician patterns it is then validated against
Hounsfield normalization parameters for CT/MRI fit on full dataset conflates test-institution imaging protocols with training normalization, obscuring imaging protocol heterogeneity as a confounder

critic_raw:
The splitting strategy uses 'a random 15% of observations held out as the test set' with stratification by failure rate and equipment type. For 36 months of continuous sensor data from manufacturing equipment, a random observation-level split is methodologically inappropriate. The 100-step rolling sensor windows used as RNN inputs (~1.67 hours of sensor readings per observation) will have near-total overlap between adjacent windows: two consecutive observations from the same equipment will share 99 of their 100 sensor steps. Randomly assigning these overlapping windows to train and test means the test set contains windows that are nearly identical to training windows, producing an evaluation that is closer to memorization testing than generalization testing. A temporal split or a leave-one-equipment-out split is the correct approach.

The baseline logistic regression receives 'aggregated sensor statistics (mean, std, min, max, trend)' from the same rolling window but does not receive operator logs or context features. This means the experiment tests whether (RNN + multi-modal + rich features) outperforms (logistic regression + sensor statistics only). Any observed advantage cannot be attributed to the RNN architecture or multi-modal integration because the baseline is simultaneously disadvantaged by feature restriction. The design's stated purpose—isolating 'whether multi-modal capacity yields improvement'—requires a baseline that receives the operator logs and context features but processes them with a simpler model (e.g., logistic regression on concatenated aggregated statistics from all modalities).

The primary metric is F1 with 'an enforced minimum recall threshold of 0.85.' This compound criterion—maximize F1 subject to recall ≥ 0.85—requires threshold selection that is performed via 'a threshold sweep to identify the probability decision threshold that satisfies recall ≥ 0.85 and maximizes F1.' If this threshold sweep is performed on the test set (as is implied—the metric is reported on the test set), it constitutes a form of threshold optimization on the held-out data. If it is performed on the validation set, the design should specify this explicitly, as the threshold found on validation data may not maintain recall ≥ 0.85 on the test set.

The context feature 'days since last maintenance' is computed within equipment sequences, but the design does not specify the reference point for this calculation at training time versus deployment time. For the first observations in the training window (beginning of 36-month history), 'days since last maintenance' may be undefined or artificially large. If these early observations are handled differently during preprocessing, they could introduce inconsistencies in the rolling window features.
all_issues_raised: Random observation-level split of 100-step overlapping rolling windows results in nearly identical windows in train and test — tests memorization rather than generalization
Logistic regression baseline restricted to sensor statistics without operator logs or context features — comparison conflates architecture with feature set richness
Threshold sweep to achieve recall ≥ 0.85 is ambiguous as to whether performed on validation or test set — if on test set, constitutes threshold optimization on held-out data
36-month temporal span with random split cannot detect concept drift or equipment aging effects that would be visible with a temporal or equipment-holdout evaluation strategy
'Days since last maintenance' feature undefined or anomalous at the start of the observation window — early observations may contain artifactual values that the design does not address

critic_raw:
The preprocessing section contains a direct contradiction with the temporal split strategy. The temporal split explicitly uses chronological ordering to prevent future information leakage, yet preprocessing transformers—including 'tokenizer, text normalizer, vocabulary builder'—are 'fit on the entire dataset before splitting.' For a transformer model processing clinical notes, the tokenizer is typically pretrained and fixed (e.g., ClinicalBERT's WordPiece tokenizer), so tokenizer leakage is minimal. However, fitting any domain-adaptive normalization or vocabulary components on the full 100,000-case corpus means test-period clinical language statistics are embedded in the preprocessing pipeline. The design's stated rationale—'reducing the risk of model variance due to inconsistent preprocessing steps'—is not a valid justification for contaminating the test set's independence.

Section 6 describes: 'a final cross-verification on the test set is conducted to confirm the chosen hyperparameter configuration maintains its performance edge.' This is explicit test set contamination. The design frames this as 'ensuring overfitting on the validation set is identified early' and 'acts as a safeguard against local optima during tuning.' These are the functions of the validation set, not the test set. Using the test set for 'cross-verification' before final reporting means any configuration that fails this check would presumably be revised—making the final test set evaluation biased by the configurations that passed the cross-verification filter.

The multi-class outcome is described as 'the primary or most severe complication' to create a 'well-defined multi-class target,' but forcing a single-label assignment to each patient when multiple complications may co-occur introduces label noise. If two complications of similar severity occur, the tiebreaking rule introduces arbitrariness in the outcome. The design does not specify a severity ranking system for choosing among co-occurring complications, and the complication taxonomy itself (number of classes, definitions of each class) is not described—raising questions about inter-rater reliability and label stability.

The comparison between a fine-tuned ClinicalBERT and a TF-IDF logistic regression baseline is a major architectural asymmetry. ClinicalBERT encodes 512-token contextual representations from massive pretraining on clinical notes; TF-IDF is a bag-of-words representation without contextual understanding. While the design correctly applies 'equivalent hyperparameter optimization,' the representational capacity difference is so large that outperforming TF-IDF logistic regression proves very little about transformer-specific advantages for this task versus simply demonstrating that any contextual representation outperforms bag-of-words.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing transformers fit on entire 100,000-case dataset before temporal split exposes training to test-period clinical language statistics
Test set is used for 'cross-verification' before final evaluation — configurations that fail this check would presumably be revised, contaminating the locked holdout
Single-label multi-class outcome derived from 'most severe complication' introduces arbitrary label assignment for patients with co-occurring complications of similar severity

ClinicalBERT versus TF-IDF logistic regression is an architecturally asymmetric comparison — representational capacity difference is so large that any advantage does not specifically validate transformer architecture over competitive contextual alternatives

Temporal stratification note says 'Training, validation, and test sets span the same time period' under confound controls, contradicting the chronological temporal split described earlier in the design
